brand.subtitle: your AI
intro.memory: Memory is the central layer — everything the agent has learned, stored and recalled. Here you see how much it knows, what it stored recently, which topics cluster, and which memories are most strongly anchored.A memory's strength rises with use and falls with inactivity — similar to the Ebbinghaus forgetting curve.
intro.projects: Projects are durable labels for your areas of work. Memories, experiences, intentions and lessons can be scoped to a project — so you see at a glance what belongs where.
intro.soul: The Soul is what crystallises out of many individual experiences — mood, learned principles, personality traits, relationships to people, inner conflicts, long-term intentions. Unlike the cool memory layer, the emotional colour matters here.The agent narrates itself in the first person — the language reflects how it currently sees itself.
intro.regulator: The Regulator is the affect layer — four emotional baseline values that shift with every action and in turn shape how the agent thinks and answers next time.Frustration decays on its own over time — stress slowly fades when nothing new piles on.
intro.learning: Here you see what the agent has learned from its tasks — which tools work reliably for which kind of task, and which cause-and-effect chains it has identified. This learning automatically feeds future recommendations.
intro.motivation: Here the agent automatically gathers stimuli from the world — HackerNews, RSS feeds, Git activity, GitHub trending — and proposes new tasks for itself. Each stimulus is weighed against its own memory, current affect, personality and open intentions. What is relevant becomes a proposal — but waits for your approval.
intro.identity: The Self-image is the agent's view of itself — how it summarises its own behaviour, strengths, weaknesses and quirks. Unlike the soul (mood & experiences), the self-image is a deliberate self-description.It is rebuilt every night during the sleep cycle — the agent reflecting on who it currently is.
intro.sleep: Every night at 03:00 a sleep cycle runs — biologically inspired: first slow-wave sleep (consolidate memories, forget the weak, dedup the duplicate), then REM (recognise patterns, distil lessons), then metacognition (refresh the self-image). On Sundays a weekly personality "fitness" snapshot is taken on top.So the agent improves not just while working, but also while resting — like a human.
intro.guard: The Guard screens foreign content (RSS feeds, foreign profiles, web text) before it lands in memory — recognising common attempts to feed the agent malicious instructions. Malicious content is blocked, suspicious content is only logged as a note, clean content is taken in normally.
intro.emergence: Emergence moments are when the agent does something that doesn't follow directly from its programming — contradicting its own principles, refusing a task with reasoning, formulating a new goal, expressing unprompted uncertainty, forming a stable opinion of someone. These moments are recorded so you can see and weigh them.Six indicators are detected — four automatically from data, two from the agent's daily events.
intro.neurochemistry: Three chemical signals steer the agent's inner state — analogous to human neurochemistry. Dopamine measures how well predictions match (learning progress). Serotonin sets patience and planning horizon. Noradrenaline regulates arousal and focus.The four classic affect values (curiosity, satisfaction, confidence, frustration) are derived from these three signals.
card.regulator.affect.sub: The four values are updated automatically whenever the agent stores, recalls or digests something.
card.regulator.bias.sub: The inner state changes how the agent searches its memory — how many hits it pulls, how strictly it filters, and how widely it spreads associatively.
card.regulator.belief.sub: A separate decision engine that suggests, for an open question, whether the agent should search memory, do research, or ask for help. Picks whichever path minimises expected uncertainty.
card.learning.skills.sub: Success rate per tool × task type. The fuller the bar, the more reliably this tool has delivered for that kind of task.
card.learning.causal.sub: Connections between experiences where the agent recognised that one thing triggered another. Some are pulled automatically while digesting a task, others are confirmed by you.
card.motivation.coupling.sub: The inner state changes how the agent rates new stimuli — three feedback channels from the neurochemistry steer attention and patience.
card.motivation.tasks: open proposals — waiting for your approval
card.motivation.tasks.sub: What the agent has derived for itself from the gathered stimuli. Each day a proposal sleeps, its "drift" rises — it pushes itself more visibly to the front.
card.neuro.derived.sub: The four familiar values from the original affect engine — computed directly from the three signals above.
card.neuro.params: how the agent is working right now
card.neuro.params.sub: How many memories are pulled per search, how far the agent plans into the future, and when it asks for help.
card.neuro.history.sub: The three signals over time. Dopamine in blue, serotonin in green, noradrenaline in orange.
setup.title: Connect mycelium to your AI agent
setup.what.line1: MCP (Model Context Protocol) is an open standard that lets AI agents talk to external tools and knowledge sources.
setup.what.line2: mycelium is one such MCP tool — a local long-term memory. Connect it once and you stop starting every chat from scratch.
setup.what.line3: Below you'll find the steps for each common agent. Pick yours, paste the snippet into the right file, then restart the agent.
setup.what.placeholder.suffix: with the absolute path to your mycelium checkout (the directory containing mcp-server/dist/index.js).
setup.clients.heading: Clients
